For example, in Genesis 13:16, God promises Abraham, "I will make your seed (*zirʿakha*) as the dust of the earth," where *zrʿ* refers to countless descendants. Similarly, in Genesis 3:15, the "seed of the woman" (*zarʿah*) is the promised line that will crush the serpent's head, using the same root for physical progeny.

This single Hebrew root replaces multiple English words depending on context, including "descendants," "offspring," "children," "progeny," "posterity," and "race" or "family line." In agricultural contexts, it can also mean "grain" or "sown crop" (e.g., Leviticus 26:5). Figuratively, *zrʿ* extends to spiritual lineage, such as "seed of Abraham" (Isa 41:8) for the covenant people. Strong's H2233 documents 229 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

זרעה.explanation:
The root *zrʿ (sbl)* means "to sow, scatter seed" in its most concrete sense, with BDB defining it as "to sow, become pregnant, conceive." As a noun, *zeraʿ* specifically refers to "seed" used for planting, as in Genesis 1:11, where God commands the earth to bring forth plants "whose seed is in itself," or to the act of sowing itself, as in Isaiah 55:10, where rain makes the earth "bring forth and bud, that it may give seed to the sower and bread to the eater." The word also appears in agricultural contexts like Leviticus 26:16, describing wasted "seed" sown in vain.

Abstractly, *zeraʿ* extends to signify "offspring, descendants, posterity" as a genealogical term, replacing English words such as "children, progeny, lineage, race, family." In Genesis 3:15, God speaks of the "seed" of the woman, and in 2 Samuel 7:12, God promises David a "seed" to succeed him. It can also mean "semen" (Leviticus 15:16) or, metaphorically, "time of sowing" (Genesis 45:6). Strong's H2233 documents 230 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ישופך.explanation:
The root *shwph (7779)* means “to bruise, crush, or strike” in its most concrete sense, describing a physical, often violent, act of pressing or smashing. The BDB lexicon defines it as “to press, crush, wound, or gore.” This verb appears famously in the protoevangelium of Genesis 3:15, where God declares that the offspring of the woman will *crush* the serpent’s head (“ישופךֹ ראש”), while the serpent will *strike* the offspring’s heel. It is used elsewhere in contexts of predatory attack, such as in Job 9:17, where Job laments that God *crushes* him with a storm (“ישופני בסערה”).

Abstractly, the single Hebrew root *shwph* replaces multiple English concepts including “crush, bruise, strike, gore, wound, smash, press, and assault.” Its semantic range moves from literal physical violence (a serpent biting a heel) to metaphorical oppression or divine judgment. Strong‘s H7779 documents 3 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ראש.explanation:
The root *r’sh (ra’sh)* means "head" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "head" as the topmost part of the body, the seat of life and intellect. In the Hebrew Bible, this word frequently denotes the literal head of a person or animal, as when David cuts off Goliath’s head (1 Sam 17:51) or when the Philistines bring Saul’s head before Dagon (1 Chr 10:10). It also refers to the top or summit of a physical object, such as the "head of the mountain" (Exod 17:9) or the "head of the ladder" in Jacob’s dream (Gen 28:12).

Abstract extensions include "chief," "leader," "beginning," "first," "best," "sum," and "company" or "division" of people. This single Hebrew root replaces a wide range of English terms: "head," "chief," "top," "leader," "captain," "first," "beginning," "sum," "choicest," and "foremost." Strong’s H7218 documents 598 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ואתה.explanation:
The root *'attâ (c/859 a)* means "you" in its most concrete sense (masculine singular). The BDB definition describes it as the second person masculine singular independent pronoun, functioning as the subject of a clause. In the Hebrew Bible, this word is used to directly address a male individual, as when God calls to Moses from the burning bush: "And He said, Do not come near here; take your sandals off your feet, for the place on which *'attâ* stands is holy ground" (Exodus 3:5). It also appears frequently in dialogues, such as when Jonathan tells David, "You (*'attâ*) will be king over Israel, and I will be second to you" (1 Samuel 23:17).

The abstract extensions of this word replace a range of English pronouns and forms based on context and translation: "you," "thou," "yourself," and sometimes "you yourself" for emphasis. Strong's H859 documents approximately 1,100 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תשופנו.explanation:
The root *šwp (שופנ)* means "to crush, bruise, or strike" in its most concrete sense, primarily denoting a violent physical action. BDB defines it as "to snap at, to bite, to crush, or to trample." In the Hebrew Bible, this verb appears in the famous protevangelium of Genesis 3:15, where God declares that the serpent's seed will *šwpḥ* (crush) the heel of the woman's seed, and he will *šwpḥ* (crush) the serpent's head. The word is also used in Job 9:17, where Job laments that God "crushes me with a tempest and multiplies my wounds without cause."

The root *šwp* carries several abstract extensions, replacing English words like "bruise," "trample," "strike," "snap at," and "pound." These nuances range from the literal crushing of a head underfoot to the figurative sense of violent oppression or destruction. Strong's H7779 documents 4 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

עקב.explanation:
The root *\`qb* means "heel" in its most concrete sense, referring to the back part of the foot. BDB defines it as "heel" (of a person or animal). This word is used in Genesis 49:17 to describe Dan as a serpent biting the horse's heels so that the rider falls backward, and in Job 18:9 where it speaks of a trap seizing the heel of the wicked.

Abstract extensions of this root include "track," "footstep," "hind-part," and "rear" (as in the rear of a formation). It also develops into the sense of "end" or "result" (e.g., the heel being the last part of the foot), and by a common wordplay in Genesis 25:26, it gives the name Jacob, who grasped his brother's heel. Strong's H6119 documents 13 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

הרבה.explanation:
The root *rbh (רבה)* means "to be or become many or much" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "be or become great, much, numerous." As a verb, the piel form *hirbah* means "to multiply" or "to make many." For example, in Genesis 1:22, God blesses the creatures saying "Be fruitful and *hirbu* (multiply) and fill the waters," and in Deuteronomy 1:10, Moses recalls that "the LORD your God has *hirbah* (multiplied) you, and behold, you are today as the stars of heaven in number."

This single Hebrew root replaces a cluster of English words including "multiply," "increase," "become many," "be abundant," "do much," and "magnify," depending on the verb stem and context. Strong's H7235 documents 276 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ארבה.explanation:
The root *rbh* means "to be or become many, much, numerous" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "to be or become many, abound, increase." This verb describes both numerical and quantitative growth. For example, in Genesis 1:22, God blesses the sea creatures, saying "Be fruitful and *rbh*," commanding them to multiply and fill the waters. In Exodus 1:7, the Israelites "*rbh* exceedingly" in Egypt, describing their rapid population increase that alarmed Pharaoh.

This single Hebrew root replaces several English words depending on context: be plentiful, increase, multiply, become great, abound, become numerous, and be many. It also carries abstract extensions like "to be great in number or degree" (e.g., of sins in Genesis 18:20). Strong's H7235 documents 226 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

עצבונך.explanation:
The root *9sb (atsev)* means "to labor, toil, or grieve" in its most concrete sense, with BDB defining it as "labor, pain, sorrow." The word *atsevonekha* (עצבונך) appears in Genesis 3:16, where God tells Eve, "I will greatly multiply your pain (*atsevonekha*) in childbirth; in pain you shall bring forth children." This concrete usage directly links the root to the physical agony of labor. Similarly, in Genesis 3:17, the ground is cursed "because of you; in pain (*atsevon*) you shall eat of it all the days of your life," tying the same root to the toil of agricultural work.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces English words such as "pain, sorrow, toil, labor, grief, hardship, and trouble," spanning both physical suffering and emotional distress. It reflects a semantic range that merges the concrete experience of hard work with the abstract weight of sorrow. Strong's H6093 documents 4 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

והרנך.explanation:
The root *hrnk (hrnk)* means "thy neck" in its most concrete sense, specifically referring to the back or nape of the neck of a person or animal. BDB defines it as "neck, back of the neck." This feminine noun appears with a pronominal suffix, modifying the direct object in phrases like "they have hardened their necks" (e.g., Jeremiah 7:26), where the physical image of stiffening the neck depicts obstinate refusal, or in the command to "break the neck of the colt" (Exodus 13:13) when redeeming a firstborn donkey.

Abstractly, *hrnk* replaces English words such as "neck, nape, stubbornness, obstinacy, defiance, rebellion, resistance, stiff-neckedness, pride, and insolence." The concrete physical term extends to metaphorically describe a person's willful and unyielding character, especially in disobedience to God. Strong's H6203 documents 38 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

בעצב.explanation:
The root *b-tsb (bets-)* means "to pain, to grieve" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines the noun *b-tsb* as "pain, toil, sorrow." In the Hebrew Bible, this word is used in Genesis 3:16-17, where God tells the woman she will bring forth children *b-*tsb* ("in pain") and curses the ground for Adam's sake, saying he will eat from it *b-*tsb* ("in toil"). Another example is Proverbs 5:10, warning that strangers may be filled with your wealth and your labors (literally "your *b-*tsb*") may go to the house of a foreigner.

Abstract extensions of *b-tsb* replace several English concepts related to the burden of hardship. This single Hebrew word covers "pain," "toil," "labor," "sorrow," "grief," and "hurt," bridging physical suffering and emotional anguish as well as the weariness of work. This semantic range moves from concrete physical pain (childbirth) to abstract emotional distress (grief over loss) to the drudgery of daily exertion. Strong's H6089 documents 17 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תלדי.explanation:
The root *yld (3205)* means to bear, bring forth, or beget in its most concrete sense. The BDB defines it as "to bear, bring forth, beget" of children. In the Hebrew Bible, תלדי appears as a 2fs prefix form, meaning "you (f.) will bear" or "you give birth." For example, in Genesis 16:11, the angel tells Hagar, "Behold, you are pregnant and *teledi* a son," directly commanding the act of childbirth. In a different context, Genesis 17:17 uses the root for Abraham's laughter, "Shall a child be born to a man who is a hundred years old? And shall Sarah, who is ninety years old, *teled*?"—here indicating the miraculous begetting of Isaac.

Abstract extensions of *yld* replace multiple English words, including "to father," "to bear," "to produce," "to cause to bring forth," and "to be in labor." The root can describe the generation of peoples (e.g., "the sons of Jacob who were born to him in Paddan-aram"), the origin of nations (e.g., "these are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created"), and even metaphorical birth of calamity or pain. Strong's H3205 documents approximately 500 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

בנים.explanation:
The root *bnh (ב-נ-ה)* means "build" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "to build, rebuild, establish, cause to continue." This word appears in the Hebrew Bible primarily to describe the physical construction of structures, as in Genesis 4:17 where *wayyiqra’ et-shem ha’ir k’shem b’no, khenokh* (he called the city after the name of his son, Enoch) but the verb *bnh* is used for Cain "building" the city. A second concrete example is 1 Kings 6:1, where Solomon *wayyiven et-bayit YHWH* (built the house of the LORD) in the fourth year of his reign, referring to the physical erection of the Jerusalem Temple.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces multiple English words: "build" (physically), "rebuild" (restore), "establish" (found a dynasty or covenant), "cause to continue" (prolong existence), and "reproduce" (in the sense of building a family through children). The root also extends metaphorically to "edify" or "strengthen" a community, as in building up a household or a nation. Strong's H1121 documents 376 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ואל.explanation:
The root *ʾul* means "to lead" or "to be in front" in its most concrete sense, as reflected in BDB’s definition: "a sign of the object, with a sense of direction or motion toward." In the Hebrew Bible, the particle *And* (ואל) combines the conjunction *and* (ו) and the preposition *to* or *toward* (אל), indicating movement or orientation toward a person, place, or thing. For example, in Genesis 12:1, Yahweh commands Abram, "Go *to* (*ʾel*) the land that I will show you," and in Exodus 3:4, God calls to Moses "from the bush" (מִן־הַסְּנֶה, *min-hassəneh*) and Moses responds, "Here I am, *and* I came *to* (*ʾel*) Him."

Abstract extensions of this root include the English words "to," "toward," "into," "about," "against," "among," "between," "by," "for," "in," "near," "of," "over," "through," "under," and "upon," depending on the context in the Hebrew sentence. The particle *ʾel* (אל) alone appears over 5,500 times in the Hebrew Bible, where it marks goal, direction, or relationship, often replacing multiple English prepositions in translation. Strong's H413 documents approximately 5,500 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

אישך.explanation:
The root *ʼišš (ʼišš)* means "man, male, husband" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "man, male (as distinguished from woman, child, and God)" with a specific nuance of "husband" when paired with *ʼiššâ* ("woman, wife"). This word is used in Genesis 2:23 where Adam declares "This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh; she shall be called *ʼiššâ* ('woman') because she was taken out of *ʼišš* ('man')." It also appears in Exodus 20:17 in the commandment "You shall not covet your neighbor's *ʼišš* (wife)"—here directly meaning "husband" as the legal owner of a woman.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces multiple English concepts: "man" (generic adult male), "husband" (married male), "person" (in certain idiomatic phrases like *ʼišš ʼelōhîm* "man of God"), and even "each" or "everyone" (in distributive constructions like *ʼišš ləʼišš* "each to his neighbor"). The term carries a strong sense of individuality and social role, distinct from *ʼādām* (generic "human") or *gævær* ("strong man"). Strong's H376 documents 2,159 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תשוקתך.explanation:
The root *šwq (שוק)* means "to be in control," "to have dominion," or "to impel" in its most concrete sense. The BDB lexicon defines it as "to run, to be impetuous, to have mastery over," with a primary sense of a powerful, often restless, drive or urge. In the Hebrew Bible, the word תשוקתך appears only three times, most famously in Genesis 3:16, where God tells Eve, "Your desire (*tšwqtk*) shall be for your husband, and he shall rule over you," conveying a yearning or compelling attraction that is bound up with relational power. It also appears in Genesis 4:7, where God warns Cain, "sin is crouching at the door; its desire (*tšwqtv*) is for you, but you must rule over it," personifying sin as a predatory force with a controlling urge.

Abstract extensions of this root replace several English concepts, including "desire," "longing," "impulse," "craving," "urge," "drive," and "inclination." It captures not a gentle wish but a potent, almost instinctive force that can be either relational (between people) or adversarial (between sin and a person). Strong's H8669 documents 3 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

והוא.explanation:
The root *hwʾ (hûʾ)* means "he" or "it" in its most concrete sense, functioning as the third-person masculine singular independent pronoun. The BDB definition categorizes it as a demonstrative and personal pronoun, used to refer to a masculine antecedent previously mentioned or understood. For example, in Genesis 3:12, the man says, "The woman whom you gave to be with me, *hwʾ* (she) gave me from the tree," where it refers back to the woman. Another example is Exodus 20:2, "I am Yhwh your God," where the pronoun *hwʾ* is often implied in the divine name to affirm identity, as in Deuteronomy 32:39: "See now that I, even I, *hwʾ* (am he), and there is no god besides me."

Abstract extensions of this root replace multiple English words depending on context, including "he," "it," "the same," and "that one." It can also function as a copula ("is") in verbless clauses, such as "The man *hwʾ* (is) wise." Additionally, it is used in genealogies and lists (e.g., "these *hwʾ* are the generations of Noah"), and with the prefixed conjunction *w* ("and he," "and it"). Strong's H1931 documents approximately 660 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ימשל.explanation:
The root *mshl (משל)* means “to be like” or “to resemble” in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as “to be like, resemble, liken.” In the Hebrew Bible, this verb often describes direct comparison: in Psalm 144:4, “Adam is *nimshal* (like) a breath,” and in Isaiah 40:18, the prophet asks “To whom then will you *t'dam'yu* (liken) God?—or what likeness will you compare to Him?”

This single Hebrew root replaces a range of English concepts including “to be like,” “to compare,” “to use a proverb,” and “to speak in a parable.” Abstract extensions appear in wisdom literature, where *mshl* governs the act of composing proverbs (e.g., 1 Kings 5:12, “He spoke three thousand *mashal* [proverbs]”) or ruling by comparison (e.g., Job 41:26). Strong's H4910 documents 34 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

בך.explanation:
The root *b*k* does not exist as a verbal root in Hebrew; rather, the word בך is a particle formed by the prefixed preposition *b* (meaning “in,” “with,” or “by”) attached to the pronominal suffix *k* (second-person masculine singular, “you”). In its most concrete sense, this particle means “in you” or “with you,” referring to a location, containment, or accompaniment relative to the addressee. For example, in Isaiah 45:14, the phrase “God is *b*k*” (בְּךָ) means “God is in you” or “with you,” emphasizing divine presence. Similarly, in Psalm 91:14, “Because he has set his love upon me, therefore I will deliver him” is followed by “I will set him on high, because he has known my name” — the word *b*k* (בְּךָ) appears in the preceding verse (91:9) as “For you, O Yahweh, are my refuge; you have made the Most High your dwelling place,” where *b*k* conveys “in you” as a location of trust.

As an abstract extension, this single Hebrew particle replaces multiple English prepositional phrases, including “in you,” “with you,” “by you,” “among you,” and “through you,” depending on context. It also serves as an indirect object (“to you”) or instrument (“by means of you”) in poetic or prophetic speech. Strong’s H???? documents 0 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible, as the word is not a root but a compound of two grammatical elements; however, the specific form בך appears hundreds of times in the Masoretic Text (e.g., Genesis 12:3, Deuteronomy 28:10, Isaiah 45:14).

שמעת.explanation:
The root *shmʿ (shin-mem-ayin)* means "to hear" in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB as "to hear, listen, obey, perceive." The verb *shamaʿta* (you heard) appears in the simple Qal stem, most often referring to the physical act of hearing sound. For example, in Genesis 3:10, Adam says, "I heard your voice in the garden" (*shamaʿti et-qolekha*), describing the auditory perception of God’s sound. In Exodus 3:7, God declares, "I have surely heard the cry of my people" (*shamoʿa shamaʿti*), emphasizing reception of a spoken plea.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root *shmʿ* replaces several English verbs: "to hear," "to listen," "to obey," "to understand," "to perceive," and "to answer" (when context implies response). The same root can shift from auditory reception to compliance, as in Deuteronomy 6:4, "Hear, O Israel" (*shamaʿ Yisrael*), which demands both listening and obeying the following commands. Strong's H8085 documents 1,159 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לקול.explanation:
The root *qol (qwl)* means in its most concrete sense "voice, sound." The BDB lexicon defines it as "sound, voice, noise." In the Hebrew Bible, this word designates the audible product of a living being, such as the human voice, the roar of animals, or the sound of natural phenomena. For example, in Genesis 3:8, Adam and Eve hear the *qol* of YHWH God walking in the garden, and in Exodus 19:16, the *qol* of a trumpet on Mount Sinai grows exceedingly loud, signaling divine presence.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces many English words: "sound, noise, voice, cry, proclamation, thunder, rumor, report, command." It extends from a concrete auditory event to any communicated message, a divine command, or even the mere mention of something. Strong's H6963 documents over 500 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

אשתך.explanation:
The root *ʿiššāh (ʾšh)* means "woman" or "wife" in its most concrete sense. The BDB defines it as "woman, wife, female"; it is the feminine counterpart of *ʾîš* ("man"). In the Hebrew Bible, *ʾištəḵā* (אשתך) is the construct form with a second-person singular suffix, meaning "your wife." For example, in Genesis 3:16, God tells the woman, "Your desire shall be for *your husband* (*ʾîšēḵ*)," and in Genesis 24:67, Isaac takes Rebekah and "she became *his wife* (*ləʾiššâ*)." The word consistently refers to a specific female person in a marital or kinship relationship, never to a generic female.

Abstract extensions: this single Hebrew root replaces English words such as "wife," "woman," "female" (often in contrast to "man"), "married woman," and "mate." It can also imply "consort" or "partner" in covenantal contexts (e.g., Proverbs 18:22, "He who finds a *wife* finds a good thing"). Strong's H802 documents 780 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ארורה.explanation:
The root *rwr (r-w-r)* means "to be accursed, execrated" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines this verb as "to be cursed, execrated, to bring a curse upon." In the Hebrew Bible, this word appears in judicial and covenantal contexts, as in the passive participle form found in Numbers 23:7, where Balaam declares "for I have been brought from Aram to curse you," using the verb *'rwr* to describe the act of uttering a formal curse. Another example is in Joshua 6:26, where Joshua pronounces a curse *'rwr* upon anyone who rebuilds Jericho, showing the word's use in declaring divine or prophetic condemnation.

Abstract extensions of this root replace multiple English concepts such as "to curse," "to execrate," "to declare accursed," and "to bring under a ban." This single Hebrew root covers the act of invoking a curse, the state of being cursed, and the ritual or legal declaration of a curse. Strong's H779 documents approximately 8 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

בעבורך.explanation:
The root *b/5668 (bʿwrk)* means "for the sake of you" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as a combination of the preposition *b* ("in, for") and the noun *ʿwrk* ("account, cause"), literally "for your account." This word appears in the Hebrew Bible as a compound prepositional phrase, typically in contexts of motivation or purpose. For example, in Genesis 8:21, God says "I will not again curse the ground *baʿburk* [for your sake] the human," and in Deuteronomy 15:10, "you shall give to him *baʿburk* [on account of you]."

Abstract extensions replace several English phrases from this single Hebrew form, including "for your sake," "because of you," "on your account," "for your benefit," and "for your cause," depending on context. Strong's H5668 documents 68 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

בעצבון.explanation:
The root *`tso`n (6083)* means "pain, toil, sorrow" in its most concrete sense, specifically the physical and emotional labor associated with human exertion. The BDB definition for *`tso`n* (עצבון) is "pain, labor, hardship." This noun appears in Genesis 3:16-17, where God tells Eve, "I will greatly multiply your pain (*`tso`n*) in childbirth," and declares to Adam, "cursed is the ground because of you; in pain (*`tso`n*) you shall eat of it all the days of your life." The word thus directly describes the painful consequences of toil and the sweat of labor.

In abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces several English concepts such as sorrow, toil, pain, hardship, labor, grief, and distress. It conveys not only physical aches but also the emotional weariness and burden of daily work. Strong's H6083 documents 3 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תאכלנה.explanation:
The root *X ʔkl* means "to eat" in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB: to consume food, devour, or partake of a meal. In the Hebrew Bible, this verb appears in the form תאכלנה, which is a third-person feminine plural imperfect (or jussive) with a prefixed ת, literally "they (feminine) will eat" or "they shall eat." For example, in Leviticus 11:34, it is used in dietary laws: "Any food that may be eaten (תאכלנה) on which water comes shall become unclean," referring to the consumption of clean food that has been contaminated. Another example is in Ruth 2:14, where Boaz tells Ruth, "Come here, and eat (תאכלנה) of the bread," inviting her to share a meal.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces a wide range of English verbs and idioms, including "to consume," "to devour," "to partake," "to feed on," "to enjoy (as in 'eat with pleasure')," and even "to destroy" (as in fire consuming a forest). It also extends to metaphorical uses like "to devour with the eyes" (envy) or "to eat words" (accept slander). Strong's H398 documents approximately 810 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וקוץ.explanation:
The root *qots* (קץ, sbl qôṣ) means in its most concrete sense "thorn, brier, or prickly plant." The BDB definition specifies a "thorn" or "brier," a sharp, woody plant that causes pain or obstruction. In the Hebrew Bible, *qots* appears most often in agricultural or judgment contexts: for example, in Genesis 3:18, God tells Adam that the ground will produce *qots* and thistles (*dardar*) as part of the curse, symbolizing the toil and frustration of farming; in Isaiah 5:6, God threatens to turn a vineyard into wasteland, letting *qots* and briers (*shamir*) grow, portraying desolation and divine punishment.

Abstractly, the single Hebrew root *qots* replaces several English words: "thorn," "brier," "bramble," "prickle," and "prickly shrub." It also carries figurative extensions for "adversary," "stumbling block," or "something worthless and painful," as in Judges 8:7, 16 where Gideon threatens to tear his enemies' flesh with *qots* of the wilderness, or Proverbs 24:31 where a neglected field is overrun with *qots* and nettles, symbolizing laziness and ruin. Strong's H6975 documents 16 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ודרדר.explanation:
The root *drdr (sbl)* means "thorn" or "thistle" in its most concrete sense, as a low, prickly weed that proliferates in abandoned or cursed ground. The BDB definition identifies it as a collective noun for briers or thistles, often associated with desolation and divine judgment. In the Hebrew Bible, it appears in Genesis 3:18, where God tells Adam, "Thorns (*drdr*) and thistles it shall bring forth for you," describing the cursed soil after the fall. It also occurs in Hosea 10:8, where the prophet warns that the high places of Israel's sin will become overgrown with thorns (*drdr*) and thistles, symbolizing ruin.

Abstract extensions of *drdr* shift from physical vegetation to metaphors for barrenness, hardship, and divine punishment. This single Hebrew root replaces the English words "thorn," "briar," "brier," "thistle," and the broader concept of "cursed growth." The word conveys not just a plant, but the idea of unproductive, painful consequence—land that yields nothing but obstacles and suffering. Strong's H1863 documents 2 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תצמיח.explanation:
The root *ṣmḥ (sbl)* means "to sprout, grow, spring up" in its most concrete sense, describing the active emergence of vegetation from the earth. BDB defines it as "spring up, grow." In the Hebrew Bible, this word is most famously used in Genesis 2:9, where God "causes to sprout" (*wayyasmach*) every tree from the ground. It also appears in the Messianic prophecy of Isaiah 61:11, where the Lord God will "cause righteousness and praise to sprout" (*yasmiach*) before all nations, using the image of a garden springing forth.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces a cluster of English verbs: "cause to grow, produce, spring forth, flourish, and bring forth." Its figurative use extends beyond agriculture to denote the rise of righteousness, deliverance, or a righteous branch (Jeremiah 23:5, Zechariah 3:8). Strong's H6779 documents 33 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ואכלת.explanation:
The root *>kl (sbl)* means "to eat" in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB. The specific form ואכלת is a qal perfect 2ms with a waw-consecutive prefix, meaning "and you shall eat." This word frequently appears in legal and narrative contexts to describe the act of consuming food, such as in Deuteronomy 8:10, "And you shall eat and be satisfied, and bless YHWH your God," or in Genesis 3:19, "By the sweat of your face you shall eat bread."

Abstract extensions of this root replace many English verbs, including "consume" (fire consuming sacrifices, Leviticus 9:24), "devour" (sword devouring enemies, Deuteronomy 32:42), "enjoy" (the fruit of one's labor, Ecclesiastes 5:18), and "waste" (time or resources, Isaiah 55:2). Strong's H398 documents 810 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

בזעת.explanation:
The root *bāzaʿ* means "to cut off" or "to gain by violence" in its most concrete sense, with BDB defining it as "to cut off, to make gain by violence, to gain unrighteously." The noun *bezat* (בזעת) appears in a single passage—Isaiah 10:19—where it refers to "the remnant of his trees" being so few that "a child could write them down" (literally, "a youth could count them") after a devastating judgment. The context involves the felling of the Assyrian forest, linking the concrete act of cutting to the aftermath of destruction.

Abstractly, this root replaces English words like "plunder," "gain," "cut off," and "estimate," capturing both aggressive acquisition and numerical reduction. Strong's H2188 documents 1 occurrence across the Hebrew Bible.

אפיך.explanation:
The root *ʼp (ʼalep-pe)* means "to be angry" in its most concrete sense, specifically referring to the flaring of the nostrils as a physical display of wrath. BDB defines the noun *ʼap* as "nostril, nose, face," from which the concrete sense of "anger" (lit., "snorting through the nostrils") derives. In the Hebrew Bible, this word appears in passages such as when God's *ʼap* burns fiercely against Israel (e.g., Exod 32:10, where מֶה-יִחַר-אַפִּי "let my wrath burn hot" uses אפי) or when human anger is described, as in Cain's burning face in Gen 4:5.

This single Hebrew root replaces multiple English concepts including "anger, wrath, nostril, nose, face, countenance, and snout," depending on context. The form אפיך specifically combines the prefix א (first person singular suffix "my") with the root, meaning "my nose/face/anger" and is often used in direct address in divine speeches. Strong's H639 documents over 770 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לחם.explanation:
The root *lḥm* (לחם) means "food" or "bread" in its most concrete sense. The BDB definition lists "bread, food, grain, loaf." This word is the standard term for bread as a staple in the Hebrew Bible, such as when Abram brings bread and wine to Melchizedek in Genesis 14:18, or when the manna from heaven is described as "bread from the Lord" in Exodus 16:4, sustaining the Israelites in the wilderness.

This single Hebrew root replaces several English words depending on context: "bread," "food," "loaf," "meal," and "provision." It can also refer to specific types of bread, like "showbread" (Exodus 25:30) or "bread of affliction" (Deuteronomy 16:3). Strong's H3899 documents 297 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

עד.explanation:
The root *ʿd (5704)* means "perpetuity, endurance" in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB. In the Hebrew Bible, this particle functions primarily as a preposition meaning "until" or "as far as," denoting a spatial or temporal limit. For example, in Genesis 8:5, the waters decrease "until (*ʿad*) the tenth month," marking a specified endpoint. It also appears in Psalm 72:7, where the righteous flourish "until (*ʿad*) the moon is no more," emphasizing an extended duration.

In abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces multiple English words and phrases, including "while," "as long as," "during," "beyond," "up to," "still," "again," and "ever." It can also convey the idea of "eternity" when paired with another term, as in *ʿad-ʿolam* ("forever"). Strong's H5704 documents 127 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

שובך.explanation:
The root *šwb (shub)* means "to turn back" in its most concrete sense, encompassing physical movement, change of direction, or return to a prior location. BDB defines it as "turn back, return." In the Hebrew Bible, this word frequently describes literal return, as in Genesis 33:16, where Esau "returned" (*šub*) to Seir after meeting Jacob, or physical withdrawal, as in Exodus 14:27, when the waters of the sea "returned" (*šub*) to their normal state and covered the Egyptians.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces a wide array of English concepts: to repent (turn from sin), to restore, to answer (turn a response), to bring back, to apostatize, to retreat, to recover, and to cause to return. The verb appears in many forms—qal, niphal, hiphil, and hophal—covering intentional action, causation, and passive states. Strong's H7725 documents over 1,050 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ממנה.explanation:
The root *mnh (mem-nun-he)* means "to count, appoint, assign" in its most concrete sense. The BDB defines it as "to count, number, reckon, assign, appoint." In the Hebrew Bible, the word *mimmennah* (ממנה) is a particle formed by the prefixed preposition *min* ("from") combined with the suffix *-ah* ("her" or "it"), but its root meaning governs the context. For example, in Numbers 3:39, the Levites are counted (*mimmennah* refers to "from their number"), and in 1 Kings 20:25, it describes appointing replacements "from their number" after battle.

Abstract extensions of this root shift from concrete counting to broader ideas of designation and allocation. English words that replace this single Hebrew root include "from her," "from it," "some of her," "of her number," "by her appointment," and "out of her portion." These reflect the particle's role in expressing source, partitive sense, or origin. Strong's H4480 documents over 5,600 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible, making it one of the most frequent particles, though the root *mnh* itself appears in fewer contexts as a verb.

לקחת.explanation:
The root *l-q-ḥ (laqach)* means “to take” in its most concrete sense. The BDB definition is “to take, take away, receive, take in marriage.” In the Hebrew Bible, this verb appears in Genesis 24:33 when Laban says, “Eat,” but Abraham’s servant replies, “I will not eat until I have spoken what I have *laqach* (taken)”—that is, until he has received an answer. It also occurs in Exodus 9:24, where hail *laqach* (takes) every herb of the field, meaning it destroys or carries them away. Thus, the word describes physical taking, receiving, or seizing.

In abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces many English words: “take,” “receive,” “acquire,” “take away,” “carry off,” “capture,” “choose,” “fetch,” “bring,” “accept,” and “take in marriage.” These nuances cover taking an object (Exod 4:20), taking a wife (Gen 4:19), taking a census (Num 1:2), taking revenge (Ezek 25:15), and even taking a journey (Gen 46:29). Strong’s H3947 documents 965 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

תשוב.explanation:
The root *šwb (sbl)* means "to turn back" in its most concrete sense, describing physical motion of returning to a point of departure. BDB defines it as "turn back, return." In the Hebrew Bible, this verb frequently describes spatial return, such as in Genesis 31:3 where YHWH tells Jacob, "שוב" (*šwb*) "to the land of your fathers," or Exodus 4:18 where Moses asks Jethro, "let me return" (*'ašubah*) to his brothers in Egypt. The foundational image is one of going and then reversing direction.

This single root replaces dozens of English verbs depending on context, converting the concrete "turn back" into abstract acts of restoration, repentance, and reversal. It can mean "answer" (as in returning a word), "repent" (returning to God, e.g., Hosea 14:2), "restore" (returning something to its owner), "withdraw" (turning back from battle), "reverse" (turning back a judgment), "rescue," "re-establish," "re-do," and "turn away from sin." Strong's H7725 documents approximately 1,060 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

שם.explanation:
The root *sm (shem)* means "name" in its most concrete sense, referring to a designation or identifier for a person, place, or thing. BDB defines it as "name," "renown," or "fame." In the Hebrew Bible, *sm* is used literally for personal names, such as Adam giving names to all the animals ("and whatever the man called every living creature, that was its *sm*," Gen 2:19), and for place names like "Beersheba" (Gen 21:31). It can also denote a memorial or monument, as when Absalom erects a pillar and calls it by his *sm* (2 Sam 18:18).

In its abstract extensions, *sm* expands to signify reputation, honor, glory, or character, often tied to God's own nature—e.g., "the *sm* of the LORD" signifies his manifested presence and power (Deut 28:58). This single Hebrew root replaces English words such as "name," "fame," "renown," "memorial," and "authority." Strong's H8034 documents over 860 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

אשתו.explanation:
The root *ʼiššâ (ʼšh)* means "woman, wife, female" in its most concrete sense. The BDB definition specifically denotes "woman, wife, female." This word is used to refer to a woman as a female human being, as when Eve is called "the woman" (*hāʼiššâ*) in Genesis 2:22, and to a wife in a marital context, such as when Abraham refers to Sarah as "my wife" (*ʼištî*) in Genesis 12:13.

In abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces the English words "woman," "wife," "female," "mate," and "bride." Strong's H802 documents 781 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

חוה.explanation:
The root *ḥwh* (2332) means "to live" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "life" or "living being," specifically denoting the vital principle of animate existence. This word is used as a proper name for the first woman, Eve, in Genesis 3:20, where Adam calls her *Ḥawwah* "because she was the mother of all living" (*kol-ḥay*). It also appears in Genesis 4:1 where Eve declares, "I have gotten a man with the help of YHWH," using the verb form *qaniti* to underscore the generation of life.

This single Hebrew root replaces multiple English words including "life," "living," "alive," "animal," "living creature," and the name "Eve." As a verb, it can mean "to live" or "to remain alive," while as a noun it may refer to a "living thing" or the abstract concept of "life." Strong's H2332 documents 5 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible, all in the context of the primeval history and the naming of Eve.

אם.explanation:
The root *ʾm (aleph-mem)* means "mother" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "mother (of humans, animals, and figuratively)." This noun appears frequently in the Hebrew Bible to denote the biological or maternal parent, such as in Genesis 3:20, where Eve is called "the mother of all living" (*ʾem kol-ḥay*). It also describes animal mothers, as in Deuteronomy 22:6, which commands not to take a bird sitting on its young "with her mother" (*haʾem ʿal-habanim*).

The concrete association with nurture and origin extends into multiple abstract or metaphorical English concepts that this single Hebrew root replaces. It can mean "ancestress" (e.g., "mother of nations" in Genesis 17:16), "source" or "origin" (e.g., a city as a "mother" in 2 Samuel 20:19), and even "clan" or "homeland" (e.g., "your mother's house" in Ruth 1:8). Strong's H517 documents 238 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

חי.explanation:
The root *hyh (hyh)* means "to live" in its most concrete sense, referring to the state of being alive as opposed to dead. BDB defines the adjective *ḥay* as "living, alive" (2416a). In the Hebrew Bible, it describes animate creatures, such as "every living creature" (Gen 9:10) and the "living God" (Deut 5:23), distinguishing what has breath and vitality from the lifeless. For example, in Gen 2:7, God breathes into Adam's nostrils the breath of life, and he becomes a *nepeš ḥayyah* (living soul), highlighting life as a direct divine gift.

Abstract extensions of *ḥay* expand beyond mere biological existence to encompass vitality, health, renewal, and enduring sustenance. This single Hebrew root replaces English words like "living," "alive," "fresh" (as in running water), "vigorous," and "life-giving," often in poetic or covenantal contexts (e.g., "the living water" in Jer 2:13). Strong's H2416 documents 509 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לאדם.explanation:
The root *’āḏām (l/120)* means "human being, mankind" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "man, mankind," often in a collective or generic sense referring to the species as distinct from animals or from God. This word is used in the Hebrew Bible to describe the first human created from the earth, as in Genesis 2:7, where *YHWH Elohim* forms *hā’āḏām* from the dust, and later in Genesis 1:26, where God declares "let us make *’āḏām* in our image," denoting all of humanity.

Abstract extensions of *’āḏām* replace several English concepts, including "human," "person," "people," "man," "mankind," and "everyone," depending on context. It can also carry a sense of lowliness or mortality, as in Psalm 8:5 where *’ănōš* and *ben-’āḏām* are used in parallel to emphasize human frailty. Strong’s H120 documents 562 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ולאשתו.explanation:
The root *l’sh (לאש)* means in its most concrete sense "to be strong" or "to have power," though the specific lexical form *l’sh* is rare; the noun *ishah* (woman/wife) is derived from a different root (איש, *ish*). The prefixed conjunction *v-* ("and") attaches to *l’ishto* ("to his wife"), which is the noun *ishah* in the construct state with a third-person masculine singular suffix. In the Hebrew Bible, this phrase appears frequently in narrative contexts, such as Genesis 3:17: "And to the man he said, ‘Because you listened to the voice of *your wife* (*l’ishtekha*)...’" and Genesis 12:19: "Why did you say, ‘She is my sister,’ so that I took her *to be my wife* (*l’ishti*)?"

Abstractly, this single Hebrew noun *ishah* replaces multiple English terms depending on context, including "woman," "wife," "female," and sometimes "each" in poetic parallelism. The concrete meaning of "woman" extends to the abstract concept of a marital partner, a gendered counterpart, or a feminine person, with connotations of companionship, covenant, and family. Strong's H802 documents 781 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

כתנות.explanation:
The root *k-t-n-t (3801)* means "tunic" or "coat" in its most concrete sense, referring to a long garment worn next to the skin, typically made of cloth or leather. The BDB definition describes it as a "tunic, a garment of various materials, worn by men and women, often as an undergarment." In the Hebrew Bible, it appears famously in Genesis 37:3, where Israel (Jacob) makes a *ktonet passim* (a "long-sleeved robe" or "ornamented tunic") for Joseph, setting him apart from his brothers and sparking their envy. Another concrete example is in Exodus 28:4, where the *ktonet* is part of the high priest's sacred vestments, woven of fine linen, for service in the Tabernacle.

In abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces several English nuances beyond the basic garment: it can be translated as "robe," "coat," or "tunic," and in some contexts carries implications of rank, favor, or status (as in Joseph's robe) or sacred duty (as in the priestly garments). It does not extend to metaphorical or figurative meanings in the same way as some roots, but its concrete usage across different narratives—from everyday wear to ceremonial dress—shows the range of social and religious significance. Strong's H3801 documents 29 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

עור.explanation:
The root *‘wr (sbl)* means "skin" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "skin (of men, animals, or objects) often in a state of being stripped or tanned." This word appears in contexts highlighting the physical covering of a body, whether human or animal. For example, in Job 7:5, Job laments, "My flesh is clothed with worms and clods of dust; my skin (*‘wr*) hardens, then breaks out again," emphasizing the decay of his physical form. Similarly, in Exodus 26:14, the tabernacle is covered with "a covering of ram skins (*‘wr*) dyed red" and "a covering of fine leather" (also *‘wr*), referring to the processed hides used in sacred construction.

This single Hebrew root replaces several English words, including "skin," "hide," "leather," and "covering," reflecting its broad application to both living beings and crafted materials. Strong's H5785 documents 100 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וילבשם.explanation:
The root *l-b-sh (ל-ב-ש)* means "to clothe" or "put on a garment" in its most concrete sense. The BDB lexicon defines it as "put on, wear, clothe." In the given word *wayyilbashsham* (וילבשם), the prefixed *waw* ("and") and third-person masculine singular *yi-* ("he") combine with the root and the object suffix *-am* ("them") to mean "and he clothed them." This form appears when Joseph's brothers, after repenting, are given "changes of garments," and Joseph "clothed them" (Genesis 45:22). Another concrete use is in Judges 6:34, where "the Spirit of Yhwh clothed" Gideon, implying a tangible donning of divine power.

Abstract extensions of *l-b-sh* replace many English verbs: "be clothed, wear, array, endue, cover, wrap, put on." In a figurative sense, God is said to be "clothed with majesty" (Psalm 93:1), and priests "put on righteousness" (Job 29:14). The metaphor extends to emotions or qualities that "clothe" a person, such as "shame" or "strength." Strong's H3847 documents over 150 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

הן.explanation:
The root *hn (h-n)* means in its most concrete sense a demonstrative particle of presentation. BDB defines it as "lo! behold! if, whether." In the Hebrew Bible, this word is used to draw attention to a following clause or object, often with a sense of immediacy or presence. For example, in Genesis 1:3, God declares, *"Yehi or"* ("Let there be light"), and the resulting light is introduced with *hen* in the sense of "behold, here it is." Similarly, in Exodus 3:4, when the Lord calls to Moses from the burning bush, the word appears as part of the divine address, emphasizing the present reality of the revelation.

Abstract extensions of *hn* replace multiple English words, including "if," "whether," "lo," "behold," "here," and "indeed," depending on context and syntax. It functions as a conditional particle in legal and wisdom literature (e.g., "if this is so"), a presentative interjection in narratives ("behold!"), and a rhetorical marker in prophetic oracles ("surely"). Strong's H2005 documents 106 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

כאחד.explanation:
The root *k-'-h-d (kaf-259)* means "one" or "unity" in its most concrete sense. The BDB definition identifies it as the cardinal number "one" or the adjective "united," "alike," or "together." In the Hebrew Bible, כאחד is often used adverbially to describe action performed in a unified or simultaneous manner, as in Judges 20:11, where "all the men of Israel gathered together to the city *k-'ehhad*" meaning "as one man." Another example is Exodus 24:3, where the people answer Moses "with one voice," literally "with one accord" (*be-kol 'ehhad*), emphasizing collective agreement and identical action.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces several English concepts, including "same," "together," "alike," "once," "united," "each," and "first" (when used as an ordinal). It conveys not just numerical singularity but also relational unity, simultaneity, and identity. Strong's H259 documents approximately 960 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לדעת.explanation:
The root *ydʿ (yod-dalet-ayin)* means to know in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB: "to know, to learn, to perceive, to discern." The prefix ל (lamed) marks this form as an infinitive construct, giving the meaning "to know" or "for knowing." In the Hebrew Bible, this word is used for direct, experiential knowledge, as in Genesis 4:1, "And Adam *yadaʿ* Eve his wife, and she conceived," where knowing is physical intimacy. A broader example is Deuteronomy 4:39, "Know (*yadaʿta*) therefore today, and lay it to your heart, that YHWH is God," where knowing involves intellectual recognition and commitment.

Abstract extensions of *ydʿ* include knowing, understanding, acknowledging, learning, discerning, perceiving, caring for, and having sexual relations. In English translations, this single Hebrew root replaces verbs like "know," "understand," "realize," "recognize," "consider," "pay attention," and "experience." Strong's H3045 documents 947 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ועתה.explanation:
The root *ˁth (c/6258)* means "now" in its most concrete sense, functioning as a temporal adverb indicating the present moment. BDB defines it as "now, at this time," often highlighting a decisive point in a narrative or speech. For example, in Genesis 27:2, Isaac says, "Behold, *ˁattah* (now) I am old," marking his present condition as the basis for his actions. Similarly, in Exodus 3:9, God declares, "And *ˁattah* (now) the cry of the Israelites has come to me," using the word to pivot from a description of suffering to divine intervention.

This single Hebrew particle replaces multiple English expressions depending on context, including "now," "then," "therefore," "so then," "this time" (with the definite article), and occasionally "henceforth." It often functions as a discourse marker, transitioning between narrative and exhortation, or introducing an inference or consequence. With the prefixed conjunction ו (*waw*), it becomes "and now" (as in the segment *wěˁattah*), a common formula for logical or emotional transitions. Strong's H6258 documents approximately 770 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ישלח.explanation:
The root *slh (sh-l-ḥ)* means "to send" in its most concrete sense. The BDB defines it as "to send, send away, let loose." In the Hebrew Bible, this word commonly describes the physical act of dispatching someone or something, as in Exodus 9:35, where Pharaoh does not *send* the Israelites away (*shillaḥ*) after the plagues. It is also used for sending a messenger or a hand, as in Genesis 32:4, where Jacob *sends* (*shalaḥ*) messengers ahead to Esau.

Beyond concrete sending, this root expands to many English concepts: to send away, to dismiss, to divorce, to let go, to release, to set free, to stretch out, to forsake, to cast off, and to permit to go. It conveys both the initiation of a mission and the act of releasing or rejecting. Strong's H7971 documents approximately 847 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ידו.explanation:
The root *yad (yd)* means "hand" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as the physical hand of a person or deity, often the right hand as the instrument of power and action. In the Hebrew Bible, *yad* appears in passages like Exodus 15:6, "Your right hand, O YHWH, is glorious in power," where the hand of God directly signifies his active strength in delivering Israel. Another example is Judges 5:26, where Jael "put her hand to the tent peg" to kill Sisera, emphasizing the hand as an agent of decisive, physical action.

Abstract extensions of *yad* are vast, replacing many English words including "power," "authority," "control," "side," "place," "portion," "time," "direction," "possession," "strength," "support," and "responsibility." It also conveys idioms like "at the hand of" (meaning through agency), "under the hand of" (under authority), and "into the hand of" (into one's power). Strong's H3027 documents approximately 1,618 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ולקח.explanation:
The root *lqh (lamed-qof-chet)* means "to take" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "to take, receive, take away, carry off." The specific form *welaqakh* joins the conjunction "and" (ו) to the root, meaning "and he took." This word is used in the Hebrew Bible to describe physical acts of taking, such as when Abraham "took" (*wa-yiqqakh*) the wood for the burnt offering in Genesis 22:6, or when the Gibeonites "took" (*wa-yiqqekhu*) old sacks and worn-out wineskins to deceive Joshua in Joshua 9:4.

Abstract extensions of *lqh* cover a wide semantic range that English splits into many distinct verbs. It can mean "to receive" (as when receiving a gift), "to acquire" (taking possession), "to take away" (removing), "to fetch" (going to get something), "to seize" (by force), "to marry" (taking a wife), or "to take up" (as a burden). Strong's H3947 documents approximately 970 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

מעץ.explanation:
The root *ʿts (עץ)* means "tree, wood" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "tree, wood, timber, stick, gallows." In the Hebrew Bible, the word *me-ʿts* (מעץ) is a construct form meaning "from/of wood" or "made of tree," as seen in Exodus 31:5, where Bezalel is skilled in cutting stones for setting and in carving *me-ʿts* (wood) for every kind of work. Another example occurs in Leviticus 14:49, where the priest uses cedar wood (*ʿets erez*) and scarlet wool for the purification of a house, highlighting the concrete material.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces an array of English words depending on context, including "tree," "wood," "timber," "stick," "log," "gallows," and "pole." It can also denote a "tree" as a symbol of life or judgment. Strong's H6086 documents over 300 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

החיים.explanation:
The root *ḥyy (ḥ-y-y)* means "to live" in its most concrete sense, denoting the state of being alive as opposed to dead. BDB defines it as "living, alive" in its primary usage. In the Hebrew Bible, *haḥayyim* frequently appears as the phrase "the living," such as in Genesis 3:22 where God warns that Adam must not be allowed to eat from the tree of life *ʿēṣ haḥayyim* lest he live forever, and in Psalm 36:10 where it is declared, "For with you is the fountain of life *mĕqôr ḥayyîm*."

Abstract extensions of *haḥayyim* encompass a wide range of English concepts, including "life" as vitality or existence, "living beings" as creatures, "livelihood" as sustenance or prosperity, and "lifetime" as duration. This single Hebrew root replaces English words such as life, alive, living, vitality, livelihood, lifetime, and even "the living" in a collective sense. Strong's H2416 documents 154 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וחי.explanation:
The root *chyh (2425)* means "to live" in its most concrete sense, as in having physical life or being alive. The BDB definition gives "live, have life, remain alive, sustain life, live prosperously, live forever, be quickened, revive from sickness, from discouragement, from death." This verb is used in the Hebrew Bible in the simple command to choose life, as in Deuteronomy 30:19, "I have set before you life and death... therefore choose life (*uvcharta bachayim*)" and in the narrative of Genesis 3:22, where God fears the man might "take also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever (*vachay l'olam*)."

Abstractly, *vachi* (the specific form in your game) extends to cover numerous English concepts including "to recover," "to be restored," "to flourish," "to be sustained," and "to be preserved alive." This single root replaces many English words that separate physical vitality from spiritual renewal, survival, and prosperity. Strong's H2421 documents over 280 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לעלם.explanation:
The root *LʿM (l-ʿ-l-m)* means "long duration, antiquity, futurity" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "long duration, antiquity, futurity, perpetuity, eternity." In the Hebrew Bible, this word is used to denote an indefinite, often very long expanse of time. For example, in Exodus 15:18, "YHWH will reign *lʿlm*" signifies an eternal, unending kingship. In Deuteronomy 32:7, "remember the days of *ʿlm*" refers to the ancient, distant past, the time of the patriarchs.

Abstract extensions of the root frequently replace multiple English concepts. In translation, this single Hebrew word often becomes "ever," "always," "ancient," "endless," "permanent," "eternity," "world," or "age," depending on context. It can denote the remote past (ancient), the unbounded future (forever), or the entire expanse of time (the world/age). Strong's H5769 documents 439 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וישלחהו.explanation:
The root *slh* (שלח) means "to send" in its most concrete sense, specifically the act of dispatching a person, messenger, or object with a purpose. BDB defines it as "to send, send away, let go, stretch out." The word *vyishlachehu* (וישלחהו) is a 3ms Qal imperfect with a waw-consecutive and an object suffix, meaning "and he sent him away." For example, in Genesis 45:24, Joseph sends his brothers away from Egypt: "And he sent his brothers away (*vyishlachehu*), and they went." In Exodus 9:7, Pharaoh sends investigators to see if any Israelites’ livestock died: "And Pharaoh sent (*vyishlachehu*) and behold, not even one of the Israelites’ livestock had died."

This single Hebrew root replaces many English verbs depending on context: "send," "send away," "dismiss," "let go," "release," "stretch out" (of the hand), "shoot" (an arrow), or "plant" (of a vine sending out shoots). Its abstract extensions include commissioning, expelling, forgiving (as in "letting go" of sin), or even the act of divorce. Strong's H7971 documents over 850 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

מגן.explanation:
The root *gnn (g-n-n)* means to cover or surround in its most concrete sense. BDB defines *mgn* as "shield" or "buckler," a hand-held defensive weapon often made of wood or metal. In the Hebrew Bible, *mgn* appears most famously in God’s self-description to Abraham: "I am your *mgn*, your very great reward" (Gen 15:1), and is used in parallel with *tsinah* (large shield) in Psalm 91:4: "His faithfulness is a *mgn* and buckler."

This single Hebrew root replaces many English words depending on context, including shield, protector, defense, ruler (when linked to the king as the nation’s *mgn*), and even "gift" or "suzerain" in diplomatic contexts. The semantic range covers physical armor, divine protection, and political sovereignty. Strong's H1588 documents 66 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

משם.explanation:
The root *sham (s*m)* means in its most concrete sense "there," functioning as a deictic adverb of place. The BDB defines it as "there, thither, in that place," and it frequently marks a specific location or destination in narrative. For example, in Genesis 2:8, "YHWH God planted a garden in Eden, from the east, and placed *sham* (there) the man whom he had formed"—*sham* points directly to the garden as the site of human habitation. Similarly, in 1 Kings 3:16, "Then two women who were prostitutes came to the king and stood before him—*sham* (there) the women stood," anchoring the scene spatially.

Abstract extensions of *sham* are less spatial and more grammatical: it replaces English adverbs like "there," "thither," "thence," and in some contexts, "in that case" or "at that time," serving as a locative, directional, and even temporal anchor. Because it often occurs with the prefixed prepositional *mem* (as in our word *misham*, "from there"), it also substitutes for "from that place" or "thence." Strong's H8033 documents 837 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

ויגרש.explanation:
The root *grsh (גרש)* means "to drive out" in its most concrete sense, often involving forceful expulsion. BDB defines it as "to drive out, cast out, expel." This word is used when God drives out the Canaanites before Israel, as in Exodus 23:28: "And I will send the hornet before you, which shall *yigresh* (drive out) the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from before you." It also appears in Genesis 3:24, where God *vayigaresh* (drove out) the man from the Garden of Eden, emphasizing physical removal.

In abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces multiple English terms including "expel," "thrust away," "divorce," "banish," "drive off," and "cast out." It can describe social separation, as in divorce (e.g., Deuteronomy 22:19, "he may not *legarsha* (divorce) her all his days"), or forced migration of peoples. Strong's H1644 documents 47 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וישכן.explanation:
The root *skn (shakan)* means "to settle down, to dwell" in its most concrete sense, often used of both human and divine inhabitation. BDB defines it as “to settle down, abide, dwell, tabernacle.” In the Hebrew Bible, this verb describes the Israelite settlement in the promised land, as “And they dwelt [*wayyishkenu*] from Havilah to Shur” (1 Sam 15:7), and famously of God’s presence dwelling among the people, e.g., “And let them make Me a sanctuary, that I may dwell [*weshakhanti*] in their midst” (Exod 25:8). The specific form *wayyishken* (“and he dwelt”) appears, for instance, in Gen 16:12 of Ishmael dwelling “in the presence of all his brothers.”

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root replaces several English concepts: to dwell, settle, abide, inhabit, encamp, remain, and rest. It also carries the specialized sense of God’s “tabernacling” or enduring presence, which later develops into the noun *mishkan* (tabernacle). Strong’s H7931 documents 139 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לגן.explanation:
The root *gnn (גנן)* means "to defend, cover, surround" in its most concrete sense, as seen in the usage of the noun *gan* (גן, "garden, enclosure"). The BDB lexicon defines this word as a "garden, enclosed garden." In the Hebrew Bible, *gan* appears most famously in Genesis 2:8, where YHWH plants a *gan* in Eden, a specific, walled-off space of cultivation and divine presence. Another concrete use is in Song of Solomon 4:12, where the beloved is called a "locked *gan*" (גן נעול), emphasizing the sense of a protected, enclosed area.

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root *gnn* replaces several English words, including "garden," "orchard," "enclosure," "paradise," and by extension any cultivated, protected space, though *gan* never carries the abstract sense of "defense" directly—that sense is reserved for the verb *ganan* (גנן, "to cover, protect"). Strong's H1588 documents 42 occurrences of *gan* across the Hebrew Bible.

הכרבים.explanation:
The root *k-r-b* (כרב) in its most concrete sense means "to draw near, approach," but only in its extended verbal form; the plural noun *kerubim* (כרבים) refers to a specific class of winged celestial beings serving as throne-bearers and guardians of sacred space. In the Hebrew Bible, *kerubim* are depicted as living creatures with composite forms—often with human, lion, ox, and eagle features—that surround the divine presence. For example, God is described as "riding upon a cherub" (Psalm 18:11), and in the Tabernacle and Temple, golden *kerubim* are placed atop the Ark of the Covenant, facing one another with outspread wings (Exodus 25:18–20). With the prefixed definite article *he* (ה), the form *hakerubim* (הכרבים) means "the cherubim," specifying these particular beings.

As an abstract extension, this single Hebrew root replaces several distinct English terms, including "cherubim," "the cherubim," and "the winged guardians," depending on context. The word never takes on the later, softened sense of a chubby baby angel; in the Bible it always denotes imposing, powerful intermediaries of God's glory and holiness. Strong's H3742 documents 91 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

להט.explanation:
The root *lht (ל-ה-ט)* means "blaze" or "flame" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines the noun להט as "flaming, blazing" or "the blaze (of a fire)." In the Hebrew Bible, this word appears in Isaiah 30:14, where it describes a vessel shattered with such force that "no sherd is found... to take fire (להט) from the hearth," and in Psalm 104:4, where God makes "his ministers a flaming fire (להט)."

This single Hebrew root replaces several English words including "flame," "blaze," "glow," and "heat." Strong's H3858 documents 5 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

החרב.explanation:
The root *hrb (H-R-B)* means "sword" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as a sharp cutting instrument used for slaughter and combat. In the Hebrew Bible, this word appears prominently in contexts of divine judgment and warfare, such as when Joshua unsheathes his *hrb* and encounters the commander of YHWH's army in Joshua 5:13, or when God's *hrb* is described as "devouring" in Deuteronomy 32:42.

Abstractly, the single Hebrew root *hrb* replaces multiple English words including "blade," "dagger," "weapon," and "war." It metaphorically represents violence, divine punishment, and the power of death, as when a generational curse promises that some "will fall by the edge of the *hrb*" (Ezekiel 5:12). Strong's H2719 documents approximately 410 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

המתהפכת.explanation:
The root *mthpk (מתהפך)* means "to turn, to change" in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB. The specific verb form *המתהפכת* (*hamit' hapechet*) is a feminine singular active participle with the definite article, meaning "the one who turns" or "the turning one." In the Hebrew Bible, it appears in Proverbs 17:20 describing a person who is perverse or deceitful: "The one with a crooked heart (*התהפך*) does not find good," referring to someone whose speech or ways are twisted or turned from the right path.

In its abstract extensions, this single Hebrew root replaces English words such as "to turn," "to change," "to pervert," "to overturn," "to be deceitful," and "to be crooked." The root conveys both physical turning (e.g., the turning of a situation) and moral or relational perversion. Strong's H2015 documents 12 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לשמר.explanation:
The root *šmr (שָׁמַר)* means to keep, guard, or observe in its most concrete sense. The BDB lexicon defines it as “keep, watch, preserve.” A key concrete use is in Genesis 4:9, where Cain asks, “Am I my brother’s *keeper*?” Here the participle *shomer* denotes a literal guard or watchman. In 1 Samuel 26:15, David says to Abishai, “You are a man, and who is like you in Israel? Why did you not *guard* your lord the king?” — referring to the physical protection of Saul.

Abstractly, this single root replaces English words like keep, guard, observe, preserve, watch, tend, heed, maintain, and protect. It is used for keeping God’s commandments (Deuteronomy 4:2), observing festivals (Exodus 12:17), preserving life (Psalm 121:7), and maintaining loyalty (Proverbs 4:6). Strong’s H8104 documents approximately 468 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

דרך.explanation:
The root *derek (sbl)* means a road or path in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB as "way, road, journey, direction." This word appears frequently in the Hebrew Bible to describe a literal route or thoroughfare, such as in Numbers 20:17, where Israel requests passage along the *derek* of a king, meaning the main highway through Edom, or in Judges 5:6, where the *derek* is abandoned due to dangers, referring to caravans ceasing to travel the open roads.

Abstract extensions broaden *derek* to replace several English concepts including manner, conduct, custom, journey, undertaking, and moral way of life. For example, it denotes a person's habitual lifestyle in Psalm 1:6, where the Lord knows the *derek* of the righteous, and in Proverbs 2:8, where God guards the *derek* of his faithful ones, meaning their course of action. Strong's H1870 documents 706 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

לרקיע.explanation:
The root *rqy` (רקע)* means in its most concrete sense "to spread out, stamp, beat out" as one would hammer a metal plate. The BDB lexicon defines it as "to beat, stamp, beat out, spread out." In the Hebrew Bible, this verb appears in the creation account of Genesis 1:6-8, where God makes the *raqia* (the "firmament" or "expanse") to separate the waters—the word itself implies something beaten out or spread thin, like a hammered sheet. Another concrete use is in Exodus 39:3, where gold is "beaten out" (*wayraqqu*) into thin plates to be cut into threads for the priestly garments.

Abstract extensions of this root convey the idea of a solid, extended surface or a "firmament" as a cosmic dome. Many English words replace this single Hebrew root: expanse, firmament, spread, beat out, hammer out, stretch, overlay. Strong's H7554 documents 11 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

המאור.explanation:
The root *hammā’ôr (hmm’wr)* means in its most concrete sense "the light" or "the luminary," specifically referring to a source of light. The BDB defines it as "light, luminary," often used for the great celestial bodies. In the Hebrew Bible, this word appears most prominently in the creation account, where God sets "the greater light" (*hammā’ôr haggādôl*) and "the lesser light" (*hammā’ôr haqqāṭōn*) to rule the day and night (Genesis 1:16). It also describes the light of the sun, moon, and stars as divine instruments for marking seasons and years.

This single Hebrew root replaces several English words depending on context: "light," "luminary," "lamp," "light-giver," and "light-bearer." Its usage spans physical illumination, celestial bodies, and metaphorical guidance or revelation. Strong’s H3974 documents 19 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

החיה.explanation:
The root *ḥyh (ḥy*h)* means “living thing” or “life” in its most concrete sense. In BDB, the word is classified as the feminine noun “חַיָּה” (a living creature, animal, or beast). This word is used generically to refer to any living animal, from domestic livestock to wild beasts. For example, in Genesis 1:24 God commands, “Let the earth bring forth *ḥayyah* (living creatures) according to their kind,” and in Genesis 3:1 the serpent is described as the most cunning of all the *ḥayyat hassadeh* (wild beasts of the field).

Abstractly, *ḥayyah* extends to signify any sentient creature, including humans in a collective sense, and can denote a bestial or untamed nature. In English, this single Hebrew root replaces words such as “animal,” “beast,” “creature,” “wildlife,” and “life form.” Strong’s H2416 documents 147 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וכל.explanation:
The root *wkl (wkfl)* means to be able, to have power, or to endure in its most concrete sense. The BDB defines it as "to be able, have power, to prevail." The particle וכל (*wkl*) is the conjunction "and" attached to the noun "all," functioning as a particle meaning "and all" or "everything." In the Hebrew Bible, it often appears in lists or summary statements, such as in Genesis 1:31: "And God saw *everything* (וכל) that he had made, and behold, it was very good," where it encapsulates the entirety of creation. Another example is in Exodus 39:32, describing the completion of the tabernacle work: "Thus all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of meeting was finished; and the children of Israel did *according to all* (וכל) that the LORD commanded Moses."

Abstract extensions show that this single Hebrew root replaces many English concepts, including "all," "every," "whole," "everything," "everyone," and "complete." It functions as a comprehensive particle, conveying totality, inclusivity, or exhaustiveness in a wide range of contexts—from physical objects to abstract groups. Strong's H3605 documents 5,416 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

צבאם.explanation:
The root *צבאם (sbl)* means "load, burden" in its most concrete sense, as defined by BDB. In the Hebrew Bible, it appears specifically in reference to a literal burden or load carried by a beast of burden, such as in Nehemiah 13:15, where the people are rebuked for treading winepresses and "bringing in burdens" (*צבאם*) on the Sabbath day. The word is also used in 2 Chronicles 2:17 to describe the "burden-bearers" (*צבאם*) conscripted by Solomon for the temple work, emphasizing the physical weight and labor involved.

Abstract extensions of *צבאם* expand its meaning beyond physical loads to include any heavy responsibility, obligation, or oppressive weight. This single Hebrew root replaces English words such as burden, load, cargo, weight, and task. Strong's H6635 documents 12 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

שבת.explanation:
The root *sh-b-t (shbt)* means "cease, desist, rest" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "to cease, desist, rest, keep the Sabbath." In the Hebrew Bible, this stem is most famously used for the weekly Sabbath day of rest, as in Exodus 20:8, "Remember the *shabbat* day, to keep it holy." It also appears in a more literal sense of ceasing labor or activity, such as in Genesis 2:2, where God "ceased (*shabat*) from all his work which he had made."

Abstract extensions of this root replace several English concepts, including "rest" (as in cessation from work), "Sabbath" (the holy day of rest), "to desist" (from action or speech), and "to keep holiday" (a festive cessation). The root also connotes a complete stop or interruption of ongoing activity, as in a pause or intermission. Strong's H7673 documents 73 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

וטוב.explanation:
The root *ṭôb (טוב)* means in its most concrete sense "good" or "pleasant," referring to something that is inherently beneficial, agreeable, or morally upright. The BDB defines it broadly as "good, pleasant, agreeable" in physical, social, or ethical contexts. In the Hebrew Bible, this word is used as a particle to introduce a positive condition or outcome, as in Genesis 1:4, "And God saw the light, that it was *ṭôb* (good)," or in Ecclesiastes 3:12, "I know that there is nothing *ṭôb* (good) for them except to rejoice."

Abstractly, this single Hebrew root can replace the meanings of many English words, including “good” (in moral quality), “pleasant” (in taste or experience), “beneficial” (in outcome), “best” (in comparison), and “well-being” (in state). Strong’s H2896 documents 741 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

להשקות.explanation:
The root *l h sh q (la-hashqot)* means "to cause to drink" in its most concrete sense (BDB: Hiphil infinitive construct of *sh q h*, "to give to drink"). In the Hebrew Bible, this word appears as a verbal form marking intentional irrigation or watering, as when Isaac's servants dig wells and the text describes the act of watering flocks (Genesis 29:2) or when a steward is tasked with giving the king's cup to Pharaoh (Genesis 40:11). The word emphasizes the causative action of providing liquid, not the mere consumption of it.

Abstract extensions of this root replace several English concepts: "to water," "to irrigate," "to give drink," "to provide drink," and "to cause to be drunk." It also appears in metaphorical contexts, such as making the earth drunk with rain (Job 36:28). Strong's H8248 documents 60 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

והיה.explanation:
The root *hyh (hyh)* means "to be, to become, to happen" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "to fall out, come to pass, occur, take place" with a primary focus on existence or occurrence. In the Hebrew Bible, the particle והיה (*wěhayah*) is the prefixed conjunction "and" attached to the perfect form, often meaning "and it came to pass" or "and it will be." For example, in Genesis 1:3, "ויאמר אלהים יהי אור ויהי אור" ("And God said, 'Let there be light,' and there was light"), the second והיה marks the actual coming into existence of light. Another instance is Exodus 12:12, "ועברתי בארץ מצרים בלילה הזה והכיתי כל בכור" ("For I will pass through the land of Egypt that night, and I will strike every firstborn"), where והיה introduces a future event that will certainly occur.

The abstract extensions of this single Hebrew root are broad, replacing many English words to describe states, transformations, and events: it can mean "to be" (existence), "to become" (change), "to happen" (occurrence), "to abide" (continuance), "to be made" (passive creation), and "to come to pass" (fulfillment of prophecy or narrative). Strong's H1961 documents over 3,500 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

האחד.explanation:
The root *’eḥad (’ḥd)* means "one" in its most concrete sense. BDB defines it as "one (the cardinal number), a (indefinite article)". In the Hebrew Bible, the word האחד is consistently used with the definite article to mean "the one" or a particular individual or entity. For example, in Genesis 1:5, "ויהי ערב ויהי בקר יום אחד" ("And there was evening and there was morning, one day"), but when the definite article appears, as in Song of Solomon 6:9, "אחת היא יונתי תמתי, אחת היא לאמה" ("My dove, my perfect one, is the only one; the only one of her mother"), האחד appears in masculine contexts to specify "the one" among a group.

Abstract extensions: This single Hebrew root replaces many English words depending on context, including "one," "first," "same," "each," "every," "alone," and "only." It can also function as an indefinite article ("a/an") or emphasize unity and singularity. Strong's H259 documents approximately 970 occurrences across the Hebrew Bible.

פישון.explanation:
The root *Pishon (pyšwn)* means in its most concrete sense "scattering" or "spreading" (BDB 834). In the Hebrew Bible, the word פישון functions as a toponym, appearing only as the name of one of the four rivers of Eden, described in Genesis 2:11 as that which "winds through the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold." It is a proper noun, not a common lexical item, and carries the sense of a river whose waters spread out or disperse across a region.

As a singular, fixed toponym, this root does not generate the abstract extensions typical of common Hebrew roots.
